title: The common-ion effect
definition: A **common ion** is a species already present in solution that also appears in an equilibrium — shared between a dissolved salt and the reaction. By **Le Chatelier's principle**, adding more of a product ion drives the equilibrium **back toward reactants**, so the reaction proceeds *less*. Two headline cases, one idea: a **buffer** adds the conjugate base $\mathrm{A^-}$ to a weak acid $\mathrm{HA} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H^+} + \mathrm{A^-}$, suppressing ionization so the pH holds near $\mathrm{p}K_a$; a **common ion in a saturated solution** adds (say) $\mathrm{F^-}$ to $\mathrm{CaF_2(s)} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ca^{2+}} + 2\,\mathrm{F^-}$, suppressing dissolution so far less solid dissolves than in pure water. Nothing new is happening — the equilibrium constant is unchanged. It is only that the mass-action expression is now satisfied at a smaller reaction extent, because one product started above zero. Solubility, then, is **not** a fixed property of a salt: it depends on what is already dissolved.
latex: K = Q(x)\ \text{met at smaller}\ x\ \text{when a product starts}> 0
